author: @bogiesholdtheweight
text:
Getting electricity free from plants or transpiration will never take off for the same reason that our river systems are not used to generate electricity. The privaledged few get their power, pardon the pun, by controlling the energy supply, among other things, and so favour centralised large power generation.
Fantastic post though. Transpiration could be really useful especially in countries with alot of rain particularly in winter.

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text:
It looks that way and energy cartels wouldn't disagree, but it's far more complex than that. I don't think there is any intentional obstacle to personal or localized power generation because few places prevent people from installing solar panels on their roof, and some governments even subsidize it. 
There is a practical aspect to large scale power generation and distribution when most people are living piled up on top of each others in cities, where it is cheaper to have a single dam or power plant. 
Governments rarely want to divert funds from their finely tuned money-siphoning schemes to finance large scale energy generation projects, so they're more than happy to let large corporations shoulder the cost in exchange for the bulk of the profits. 
The energy companies could afford to put a bunch of small generator along every stream but it wouldn't be as profitable, and most governments have no incentive to get entangled in the logistics of such projects.
My main issue with centralized generation is when the power is sent to distant locations as the energy losses increase with distance, and if the source of power is coal, diesel, gas or nuclear, it's a lot of waste and damage for absolutely nothing.

author: @vittoriosandoni7064
text:
Well while this is a courious idea the reality is that photosintesis is a really inefficient process, if we make the radiation that hits a leaf 100, it is divided in:

8-10% is reflected back
8-10% is transmitted trough the leaf
The remaining radiatio is absorbed (80-85%)

Of the absorbed radiation:
22% is emmitted as infrared radiatio
62% is used in traspiration/lost to convection

Finally 1-2% is used in photosinthesis.

Thit data is pubblisced in "manuale di viticoltura" by A. Palliotti, S.Poni, O. Silvestroni.
(It's a viticulture manual.)

This data is only true for leafes that are in direct sunlight, if you go inside the leaf canopy the the ratio between red (usefull) and far red(useless) radiatio gets worse, decreasing the photosinthetic yeald.

More over a leaf does not photosinthesise the same way during it's lifetime (for a grapevine leaf) the net photosinthesis grows from 0 to 150 micromoles of CO2/m*m*s
Then it stays the about the same for about 120-150 days with peaks around 160 micromoles of CO2/m*m*s

To then starts to reduce as the leaf grows old and decays.

All of the data above is refered to leafs and plants that are in good condition, without any stress.

PS english is not my first language, there will be mistakes, sorry.

text: Considering there trials made adding copper wires to the soil to supposedly help plants grow (though there hasn't been accepted by the scientific community yet, and I don't know if there has been any serious experiment conducted), I wonder if extracting electricity from the plant would be detrimental to its growth.
